coredata.dc:title: Red-emission hydrophobic porphyrin structure carbon dots linked with transferrin for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Ji
Chen, Shuai
Yu, Yong-Liang
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: A porphyrin structure carbon dots (CDs) with red fluorescence is prepared by the reaction of terephthalaldehyde with pyrrole under the trifluoroacetic acid catalyst. The CDs with an average particle size of 5.70 nm are red-emission with a maximum fluorescent wavelength at 650 nm due to the large conjugate structure of the porphyrin ring, giving rise to a quantum yield of 14%. The aldehyde groups in CDs make self-aggregation possible, and promote the effective coating of CDs around Transferrin (Trf) via the Schiff base reaction, forming CDs@Trf nanocomposites with a particle size of 19.7 nm. Trf can specifically target cells with Trf receptors on the surface of cell membrane. CDs@Trf exhibits stable red-emission in low polarity solvents, and are able to generate singlet oxygen (1O2), which facilitates cell imaging and photodynamic therapy. In vitro cell experiments demonstrate that CDs@Trf greatly inhibits the growth of tumor cells under laser irradiation at 635 nm. After incubation of 3 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf with Bend.3 cells, the survival rate of the cells is only 26% under laser irradiation of 4.78 mW cm−2 for 5 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission carbon dots
Porphyrin
Singlet oxygen
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CDs with hydrophobic porphyrin structure
Preparation and characterization of CDs and CDs@Trf
Cell imaging and viability in photodynamic therapy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CDs in DCM (insert: size distribution of CDs); (b) HRTEM lattice spacing image of CDs; (c) HRTEM image of CDs@Trf in DI (insert: size distribution of CDs@Trf); (d) SEM image of CDs@Trf; (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black) and CDs@Trf (red); (f) Raman spectrum of CDs on silicon water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
XPS survey spectra of (a) CDs and (b) CDs@Trf; C1s spectra of (c) CDs and (d) CDs@Trf.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (inset: local magnification within the wavelength of 500–650 nm), (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in DCM at various excitation wavelengths.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs@Trf solution containing 0.122 mM DPBF under 635-nm laser irradiation for different times; (b) The absorbance of the CDs@Trf solution containing 0.122 mM DPBF at 418 nm and that of 0.122 mM DPBF in DI as the control under 635-nm laser irradiation within 0–45 min.

Confocal microscopic images of Bend.3 cells incubated with 2 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf at 37 °C for 4 h. (a) Collection within 550–600 nm; (b) Collection within 600–750 nm; (c) Bright field; (d) The merge image of the fluorescence and bright field; (e) Viability of Bend.3 and MCF-7 cells incubated with different concentrations of CDs@Trf (0, 0.02, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 3 mg mL−1) in the presence and absence of 635-nm laser irradiation.
Schematic illustration of CDs synthesis and CDs@Trf for generating singlet oxygen under laser irradiation to carry out irreversible damage to the cell.

head.ce:title: Red-emission hydrophobic porphyrin structure carbon dots linked with transferrin for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110169, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110169, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A porphyrin structure carbon dots (CDs) with red fluorescence is prepared by the reaction of terephthalaldehyde with pyrrole under the trifluoroacetic acid catalyst. The CDs with an average particle size of 5.70 nm are red-emission with a maximum fluorescent wavelength at 650 nm due to the large conjugate structure of the porphyrin ring, giving rise to a quantum yield of 14%. The aldehyde groups in CDs make self-aggregation possible, and promote the effective coating of CDs around Transferrin (Trf) via the Schiff base reaction, forming CDs@Trf nanocomposites with a particle size of 19.7 nm. Trf can specifically target cells with Trf receptors on the surface of cell membrane. CDs@Trf exhibits stable red-emission in low polarity solvents, and are able to generate singlet oxygen (1O2), which facilitates cell imaging and photodynamic therapy. In vitro cell experiments demonstrate that CDs@Trf greatly inhibits the growth of tumor cells under laser irradiation at 635 nm. After incubation of 3 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf with Bend.3 cells, the survival rate of the cells is only 26% under laser irradiation of 4.78 mW cm−2 for 5 min.

Hydrophobic porphyrin carbon dots linked transferrin is used for cell imaging and in vitro photodynamic experiment.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials have been paid increasing attention due to the outstanding merits of low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and chemical stability [1]. The carbonization nucleation of materials containing carbon element is usually carried out by a heat reaction under a high temperature and pressure. Carbon dots (CDs), usually spherical or hemispherical with sizes below 10 nm, consist mainly of a core of inter sp2-hybridized carbon atoms with many functional groups on their surface [2]. They are discovered accidently during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotube [3]. Many CDs have been reported due to their ease of synthesis and eco-friendly properties, which are now outstanding representative members with the potential for use in biomedicine. However, in aqueous solution, the emission of most CDs is usually in the blue to green regions, which limits the potential of their application in the biological field due to the interference from biological self-fluorescence. Therefore, it is urgent to develop long-wavelength emission CDs for further biologically relevant applications.

In this work, we designed and prepared a hydrophobic porphyrin structure CDs with terephthalaldehyde and pyrrole as raw materials. The CDs have a large conjugate structure and emit bright and stable red fluorescence. Due to the highly symmetrical porphyrin ring structure, the CDs exhibit a lower polarity and molecular dipole moments. The synergistic effect of the conjugate structure of delocalized π bond increases the emission wavelength, improves the hydrophobicity of the materials, and makes it easier for the materials to emit stable red fluorescence in similar low polar solvents. Porphyrin rings are synthesized commonly by an aromatic compound with a blocked aldehyde para-position to avoid the generation of copolymer. However, in our study, the terephthalaldehyde was adopted as raw material and reacted with pyrrole under the trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) catalyst, by simple stirring at room temperature to obtain the red fluorescent CDs. The abundant aldehyde groups in the reactants provide the potential for polymerization. For the first time, the porphyrin structure is introduced into CDs. After Trf was combined to the hydrophobic porphyrin CDs, CDs@Trf nanocomposites with a core-shell structure were formed. The aldehyde groups on the CDs can form a Schiff base with the amino groups on the protein [28,29], making them firmly bonded together. The CDs@Trf nanocomposites could be self-assembled in water, resulting in the disappearance of the fluorescence, but when transported into the cell by the protein, they could still emit the fluorescence and thus illuminate the cell. In addition, the CDs@Trf nanocomposites have photodynamic properties that can produce singlet oxygen under light irradiation, thus killing cancer cells and making them applicable to PDT.

N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), pyrrole, trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), transferrin (Trf) and 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Terephthalaldehyde was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol and anhydrous methanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). For cell culture, Dulbecco's modified eagle high glucose medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum, 0.25% trypsin-EDTA and penicillin/streptomycin were purchased from Hyclone (Thermo Scientific, USA). 3-(4,5 Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit was purchased from Nanjing KeyGEN Biotech (Nanjing, China). All reagents and solvents used were of analytical reagent grade and purchased from commercial sources, and used without further purification. Deionized water of 18 MΩ cm was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs with hydrophobic porphyrin structure
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs with a porphyrin ring structure were synthesized for the first time at room temperature via a one-step method using simple stirring. First, 0.1341 g of terephthalaldehyde was dissolved in 25 mL of dimethyl formamide (DMF) to form a transparent and homogeneous solution, followed by addition of 138 μL of pyrrole and 200 μL of TFA. The mixed solution was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 120 h. With the increase of reaction time, the solution changed from colorless to red and finally to black. DMF was washed off with deionized (DI) water after the reaction. A silica gel chromatographic column was used to separate and purify the CDs with dichloromethane (DCM) as mobile phase. Finally, the black-purple CDs powder was obtained via rotary evaporation and re-dispersed in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO).

All the glassware used for the reaction of CDs with Trf were soaked in fresh aqua regia (HCl: HNO3 = 3:1, v/v) for 24 h prior to the reaction, and then repeatedly flushed with DI water and dried completely. Subsequently, 200 μL of 10 mg mL−1 Trf solution and 1760 μL of DI water were added into a sample bottle under vigorous stirring. Afterwards, 40 μL of 6.6 mg mL−1 CDs were added to the dilute Trf solution drop-widely under vigorous stirring for 30 min. Eventually, a uniform and stable solution was formed, and stirring continued for 4 h to allow the coupling reaction to occur completely. Finally, a brown solid was attained with lyophilizate and stored at 4 °C.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired using a JEM-2100 (JEOL Ltd, Japan) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. 1HNMR (600 MHz) spectra operated on a Bruker Avance spectrometer, with CDCl3-d1 as solvent and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as internal standard. LC-MS analysis of the CDs was carried by Agilent-1260-6540 ESI Q-TOF with acetonitrile and methanol as solvent. MALDI-TOF-MS analysis of the CDs was carried by ultrafleXtreme MALDI-TOF/TOF MS (BRUKER Ltd, America), with DBH as matrix. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) from 500 to 4000 cm−1. Ultraviolet visible absorption spectra were measured by using a U-3900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained through an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 1 mm slit and quartz cuvettes. The excitation and emission slits are set as 5.0 nm, along with a scan speed of 1200 nm min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo, USA) with an Al Kα 280 eV excitation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were received from a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Holland) with Cu-Ka irradiation (Cu Kα = 1.54 Å) in the range of 2θ from 10 to 90°. Raman spectra were recorded on an XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba Co., France) with an excitation wavelength at 638 nm. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were obtained through a SU8010 field-emission electron microscope at 5.0 KV (Hitachi, Japan).

1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a 1O2 trapping agent to indicate the generated amount of 1O2. The fresh indicator solution was prepared by dissolving 5.4 mg of DPBF in 4 mL ethanol and stored at 4 °C. The 1O2 measurement was achieved by following the next steps in dark. First, 2 mL of CDs@Trf was scanned to serve as a baseline for UV–Vis absorption spectra, after 50 μL of DPBF stock solution was added to the UV cuvette. UV–vis absorptions were measured every 2 min by laser irradiation (635 nm, 76.43 mW cm−2) for 30 min, and afterwards the measurement was conducted every 5 min for 15 min. The absorbance at 400–440 nm was recorded. 50 μL of DPBF stock solution was added in 2 mL of DI as control group, and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured at same conditions.

The potential cytotoxicity of the CDs was tested by varying the concentrations of the CDs solutions (0, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2 mg mL−1) and co-incubating with Bend.3 cells. The potential for cellular imaging with the obtained CDs@Trf was tested by Bend.3 cells. These cells at an initial density of 1 × 105 cells cm−2 were seeded in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% (v/v) penicillin, and streptomycin at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h to adhere to the well plate. In the dyeing experiment, the original culture medium was replaced with the fresh one containing 2 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf and 0.1% (v/v) DMSO for ensuing cell incubation. After incubation for another 4 h to allow the CDs@Trf to fully enter the cell interior, the culture medium was removed and the cells were washed thoroughly for three times with phosphate buffer (PBS, 10 mmol L−1, pH 7.4). Finally, images of Bend.3 cells were taken using a laser scanning confocal microscope with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm at a magnification of 40 × and λem 550–750 nm.

To assess the damage by CDs@Trf nanocomposites to cells, MCF-7 cells and Bend.3 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (200 cells/well) to adhere for 12 h in the medium and standard culture mentioned in the previous section. The used medium was then removed for a new medium without FBS and containing different concentrations of CDs@Trf (0, 0.02, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 3 mg mL−1) and 1% (v/v) DMSO was added. After incubation for 4 h, each well was exposed to a 635-nm laser with a power density of 4.78 mW cm−2 for 5 min. After incubation for another 2 h, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh portion and 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT). After washing with PBS, the sample was also incubated for 4 h to form violet-colored formazan. Finally, the supernatant MTT medium was removed carefully and 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan. After shaking for 10 min, absorbance (A) at 490 nm was measured using an ELISA plate reader. The cell viability was estimated based on the following equation:
Cell Viability% =
(
OD CDs@Trf
−
OD control
)
/
(
OD cell
−
OD control
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and CDs@Trf
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the CDs were synthesized by a stirring approach at room temperature, presenting a lamellar structure with π-π stacking via self-assembly. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , terephthalaldehyde acts as a part of the porphyrin-ring providing material and only one aldehyde group is consumed in the reaction, resulting in the CDs with abundant free aldehyde groups in side chains. This increases the possibility of aggregation, but the degree of the reaction can be controlled by the reaction time, ensuring timely removal of the solvent and catalyst from the reaction system to terminate polymerization and forming the CDs with excellent luminescent properties. TFA is used as the catalyst to accelerate the formation of conjugate rings. Finally, a silica gel column is used to separate the CDs. It is observed that the obtained CDs solution emits a bright red fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm.

The morphology of CDs and CDs@Trf can be observed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. The as-prepared CDs dissolved in DCM as single spherical particle are well dispersed with a mean particle diameter of 5.70 nm (Fig. 1 a, inset). Furthermore, the CDs have lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1b) [30,31]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs shows no sharp diffraction peak and lattice structure (Fig. S1). The average size of CDs@Trf nanocomposites dissolved in DI is 19.7 nm (Fig. 1c), indicating that the CDs are encased in the inner cavity by Trf with a size of 17.39 nm in DI water (Fig. S2). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) image demonstrates the clearly visible lamellar structure of CDs@Trf nanocomposites (Figs. 1d and S3). This is due to the fact that the aldehyde groups on the surface of the CDs can react easily with the amino groups on the surface of Trf to form a Schiff base.

The functional groups on the surface of CDs and CDs@Trf were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1e). The characteristic absorption of porphyrin appears at ca. 1000 cm−1 in the functional groups of CDs and CDs@Trf. There is a broad absorption band corresponding to N–H stretching vibration at 3400 cm−1, along with CO stretching band at 1695 cm−1, indicating the presence of aldehyde groups. Since Trf is combined to CDs by an amide bond, the redundant pair of electrons in the N atom causes the electron cloud of CO to move towards C–N, thus decreasing the electron cloud density of CO and the vibration frequency to 1637 cm−1. The band of CDs located at 1600 cm−1 can be attributed to CC/CN bonds, while that at 1461 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration band of the benzene ring. The occurrence of a peak at 1539 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of the amide II band formed by coupling N–H and C–N, indicating the formation of amide bond on CDs@Trf. The absorption located at 1382 cm−1 indicates that C–N is also found on the CDs. The characteristic absorptions of porphyrin structure are found at 1018, 1022, 1153 and 1095 cm−1. Raman spectrum shows that two peaks at approximately 1539 and 1352 cm−1 (Fig. 1f) are attributed to G band and D band [32,33], respectively, indicating the presence of both sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in the CDs. Furthermore, it is observed from the 1HNMR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S4) that there is a peak with hydrogen drift of less than 0 due to the porphyrin formation and corresponding conjugate ring current of electrons.

The changes in the structure and the element content from CDs to CDs@Trf were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), demonstrating the combination of CDs and Trf (Fig. 2 and S9). Three typical peaks in XPS survey spectrum are attributed to C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (532 eV), respectively. Elemental analysis reveals a composition of C (72.99%), N (0.37%), and O (26.64%) for CDs and C (67.99%), N (14.01%), and O (18%) for CDs@Trf. The obvious increase in N content after modification is caused by the abundant amino groups on Trf. For CDs, the deconvolution of the peak at 284.5 eV is associated with sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon (sp2 CC), whereas the peak at 285.1 eV derives from sp2-hybridized CC with defects [5,34,35]. The peaks of C 1s corresponding to CO and –COOH are both observed in CDs and CDs@Trf, with locations at 285.9 and 289.3 eV. According to the results of XPS, there are various functional groups on the surface of CDs, and the appearance of carboxyl group is due to the slight oxidation of the aldehyde group in air. After CDs react with Trf to form the Schiff base, some of the aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs are consumed, thus stabilizing the CDs@Trf.

LC-MS analysis result was obtained by injecting 10 μL of 20 μg mL−1 CDs in DCM with methanol and acetonitrile as mobile phases (Fig. S5). It is clear that a molecular weight of 726 matches the integrated porphyrin structure, but that of 337 corresponds to the defected-porphyrin CDs. MALDI-TOF-MS analysis (Fig. S6) was conducted to measure the molecular weight of the CDs with 2, 5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DBH) as the matrix, further confirming that the molecular weight of the CDs is 726. The absence of the molecular weight of 337 might be due to the fact that the instantaneous high temperature cannot pyrolyze the CDs in MALDI-TOF-MS. However, electrospray ionization in LC-MS may cause the porphyrin rings break peak in 337. UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 a) shows that the nanostructure has the characteristic absorptions of porphyrin rings, a Soret absorption band located at 420 nm, and four Q absorptions at 509, 545, 586, and 644 nm. It indicates that the porphyrin ring structure is present in the CDs without the insertion of metal. Soret and Q bands are produced by π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively, thereby demonstrating the structure of porphyrin rings. The strongest absorption located at the Soret band is not the ideal wavelength for PDT due to the high photocytotoxicity and poor tissue penetration at this band. In fact, light with a wavelength of 650 nm is usually selected, because it is closer to the near infrared (NIR) window (650–900 nm) and more efficient for performing PDT [36].

As observed from the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b), the CDs exhibit obvious excitation-independent characteristics. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength of the CDs remains virtually unchanged at 650 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs is obtained from the fluorescence wavelength scanning image. When the excitation wavelength is set at 400 nm, the emission intensity of the CDs at 650 nm reaches a maximum. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength is set at 400 nm. The CDs emit red fluorescence in a low-polarity organic solvent, while fluorescence quenching is observed in pure water. The fluorescence 3D map was scanned after the CDs were dissolved in pure water. It is observed that there are almost no fluorescent signals in the visible light region due to the strong polymerization arising from the action of hydrogen bond (Fig. S8). The absolute quantum yield of the CDs is 14%.

The light-induced 1O2 generation capacity of the CDs@Trf nanocomposite as photosensitizer was measured by using DPBF as the 1O2 indicator. According to the properties of the CDs@Trf nanocomposite, there are four absorption bands within 500–650 nm. In this case, the laser at 635 nm is chosen to evaluate the singlet oxygen generation effect of the nanocomposite, due to the strong penetration ability of red light and the low impact on biological tissues. Fig. 4 a shows the degradation of DPBF under laser radiation from 0 to 45 min. With the increase of irradiation time, DPBF is degraded due to the generation of 1O2 caused by CDs@Trf and oxygen in air. Thus, the absorption at 418 nm is gradually weakened. An irradiation time-dependent decrease in DPBF absorption is observed at 418 nm upon the irradiation of CDs@Trf with a 635-nm laser (Fig. 4b). As the control group is the absence of the CDs@Trf nanocomposite, oxygen from the air hardly degrades DPBF and the absorption peak at 418 nm virtually remains unchanged, confirming the light-induced generation of 1O2 by CDs@Trf. It is proved that the CDs@Trf nanocomposite has excellent singlet oxygen generation rate under laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and viability in photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the CDs was assessed prior to cell imaging (Fig. S7). It was observed that there is almost nontoxicity to cells when the concentration of the CDs is < 2 mg mL−1. The CDs@Trf nanocomposite can easily cause cell endocytosis, when Bend.3 cells are co-incubated with CDs@Trf. Under a laser confocal microscope, the cells are observed within the bands of 550–600 nm (yellow) and 600–750 nm (red) at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm (Fig. 5 a and b). In the bright field image (Fig. 5c), the cells are spindle-shaped, indicating a good state of survival. From the merger of images (Fig. 5d), it is clear that the CDs@Trf nanocomposites have entered the cell interior and distributed mainly in the cytoplasm, due to the abundance of proteins and lipids in the intracellular fluid and the lipophilic property of CDs@Trf. It is also more conducive to the rapid entry of cells and attachment to membranous organelles.

The CDs@Trf nanocomposites exhibit high intracellular toxicity effect after entering the cells (Fig. 5e). Bend.3 cells have more Trf receptors on their surface, which would grasp more CDs@Trf nanocomposites into themselves by binding with the Trf proteins. Under 635-nm laser irradiation, more 1O2 could be generated, leading to a greater number of cells dying faster. In contrast, MCF-7 has a slightly lower mortality rate during the study period. After incubation of cells with 3 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf, only 26% of Bend.3 cells can survive under laser irradiation of 4.78 mW cm−2 for 5 min, while 47.88% of MCF-7 cells can survive under the same conditions. However, in the absence of light, the survival rates of Bend.3 and MCF-7 cells are 45.78% and 61.62%, respectively.

In this work, a hydrophobic CDs with porphyrin structure is prepared, which could emit red fluorescence in cells. The targeting of cells with Trf receptors on the cell surface is improved by linking CDs to Trf through Schiff base reaction. The obtained CDs@Trf nanocomposite can rapidly produce singlet oxygen in vitro under laser irradiation, and displays a favorable killing effect on cancer cells. After incubation of Bend.3 cells with 3 mg mL−1 CDs@Trf, the cell survival rate is only 26% under 635-nm laser irradiation for 5 min. Therefore, the CDs@Trf nanocomposite exhibits a favorable potential application in the field of photodynamic therapy.

Ji Feng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shuai Chen: Conceptualization. Yong-Liang Yu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jian-Hua Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402, 21874017, 21727811 and 21605014), Liaoning Provincial Program for Promoting Talents (XLYC1807005 and XLYC1802016) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N180504003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Q. Meng, L. Wang, J. Zhang, Y. Song, H. Jin, K. Zhang, H. Sun, H. Wang, B. Yang, Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52 (2013) 3953-3957.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Bao, Z.L. Zhang, Z.Q. Tian, L. Zhang, C. Liu, Y. Lin, B. Qi, D.W. Pang, Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism, Adv. Mater. 23 (2011) 5801-5806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, R. Ray, Y. Gu, H.J. Ploehn, L. Gearheart, K. Raker, W.A. Scrivens, Electrophoretic Analysis and Purification of Fluorescent Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube Fragments, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 126 (2004) 12736-12737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crossover between anti- and pro-oxidant activities of graphene quantum dots in the absence or presence of light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chong, C. Ge, G. Fang, X. Tian, X. Ma, T. Wen, W.G. Wamer, C. Chen, Z. Chai, J.J. Yin, Crossover between Anti- and Pro-oxidant Activities of Graphene Quantum Dots in the Absence or Presence of Light, ACS Nano 10 (2016) 8690-8699.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porphyrin-based carbon dots for photodynamic therapy of hepatoma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, X. Zheng, X. Zhang, S. Liu, Q. Pei, M. Zheng, Z. Xie, Porphyrin-based carbon dots for photodynamic therapy of hepatoma, Adv. Healthc. Mater. 6 (2017) 1600924.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phosphorescent carbon dots for highly efficient oxygen photosensitization and as photo-oxidative nanozymes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, X. Lu, D. Tang, S. Wu, X. Hou, J. Liu, P. Wu, Phosphorescent carbon dots for highly efficient oxygen photosensitization and as photo-oxidative nanozymes, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 10 (2018) 40808-40814.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diketopyrrolopyrrole-based carbon dots for photodynamic therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual functionalized natural biomass carbon dots from lychee exocarp for cancer cell targetable near-infrared fluorescence imaging and photodynamic therapy
